perturbation.original_text: 1.1. For a period of three (3) years from the date of entering in to this Agreement, not to disclosure any Information or Item, in any manner whatsoever, directly or indirectly, whether in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of Disclosing Party, to any individual, firm or entity, excepting solely an individual
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1. For a period of one (1) year from the date of entering in to this Agreement, not to disclosure any Information or Item, in any manner whatsoever, directly or indirectly, whether in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of Disclosing Party, to any individual, firm or entity, excepting solely an individual
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-disclosure period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under New York law, depending on the nature of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's statute of limitations for trade secret misappropriation is three years. A one-year non-disclosure agreement may be deemed unreasonable, especially if the trade secret remains valuable for longer than one year, potentially undermining the legal protection afforded by the statute.

Previous Next § 214. Actions to be commenced within three years: for non-payment of money collected on execution; for penalty created by statute; to recover chattel; for injury to property; for personal injury; for malpractice other than medical, dental or podiatric malpractice; to annul a marriage on the ground of fraud. The following actions must be commenced within three years: 1. an action against a sheriff, constable or other officer for the non-payment of money collected upon an execution; 2. an action to recover upon a liability, penalty or forfeiture created or imposed by statute except as provided in sections 213 and 215; 3. an action to recover a chattel or damages for the taking or detaining of a chattel; 4. an action to recover damages for an injury to property except as provided in section 214-c; 5. an action to recover damages for a personal inj
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the non-disclosure period from three years to one year, while New York's statute of limitations for trade secret misappropriation is three years. This creates an inconsistency because a one-year non-disclosure agreement may be deemed unreasonable, potentially undermining the legal protection afforded by the statute. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both referencing NY CPLR § 214, confirm that actions must be commenced within three years. Therefore, reducing the NDA period to one year directly contradicts the legal protection period, rendering the modified contract potentially unenforceable for the full duration allowed by law.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Employees. For a period of two (2) years Recipient (or any agent or affiliates of the Recipient) will not have discussions with any "Covered Employees" of the Disclosing Party regarding employment opportunities with the Recipient or any of its affiliates. "Covered Employees" will include any person who is employed by the recipient as a part or full time employee either as of the date of this Agreement, or persons that subsequently become employees of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Employees. For a period of five (5) years Recipient (or any agent or affiliates of the Recipient) will not have discussions with any "Covered Employees" of the Disclosing Party regarding employment opportunities with the Recipient or any of its affiliates. "Covered Employees" will include any person who is employed by the recipient as a part or full time employee either as of the date of this Agreement, or persons that subsequently become employees of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the no-hire period to five years may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under New York law, especially if it unduly restricts former employees' ability to find employment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York restraint of trade principles
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly contradicted, a five-year non-solicitation clause may be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade if it is broader than necessary to protect the disclosing party's legitimate business interests. New York courts scrutinize such clauses to ensure they are reasonable in time and scope.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the no-hire period from two to five years, which, according to the Perturbation Explanation, may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under New York law. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that New York courts scrutinize such clauses to ensure they are reasonable in time and scope. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address the reasonableness of time constraints in non-solicitation agreements, the general principle against monopolies and restraints of trade in NY law can be inferred. Therefore, the extension to five years creates a potentially illegal restraint, especially if it unduly restricts former employees' ability to find employment, leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: However, if a person subsequently terminates their employment with the Disclosing Party, then the recipient can have such discussions with that person, after a 5-day waiting period.
perturbation.changed_text: However, if a person subsequently terminates their employment with the Disclosing Party, then the recipient can have such discussions with that person, immediately.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the waiting period may violate non-compete agreements between the employee and the disclosing party.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 201-D primarily addresses discrimination based on legal recreational activities, New York's general approach to non-compete agreements requires a reasonable scope and duration to protect the employer's legitimate business interests. Eliminating a brief waiting period might facilitate the circumvention of a valid non-compete agreement the employee has with the disclosing party, creating a conflict.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a 5-day waiting period before a recipient can discuss matters with a former employee of the disclosing party, which could conflict with the disclosing party's non-compete agreements. While the provided snippet focuses on discrimination in child-care leave (SECTION 201-C) and maintenance of employee-patient records (SECTION 201-E), the Law Explanation highlights that New York law generally requires reasonable scope and duration for non-compete agreements to protect legitimate business interests. Removing the waiting period could allow circumvention of a valid non-compete agreement, creating a legal contradiction as it potentially violates the terms and enforceability of such agreements.

perturbation.original_text: 13.2. Validity, interpretation, performance, remedies, and all other issues relating to this Agreement shall be governed by the internal law of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.2. Validity, interpretation, performance, remedies, and all other issues relating to this Agreement shall be governed by the internal law of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California when the disclosing party is in New York may be invalid if there is no reasonable relationship between the contract and California, particularly if the trade secrets and employees are primarily located in New York.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles - New York
perturbation.law_citation: KLM Royal Dutch Airlines v. Checchi, 105 A.D.3d 909, 963 N.Y.S.2d 374 (2d Dep't 2013)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York choice of law principles, a contractual choice of law provision will generally be respected if the chosen state has a reasonable relationship to the agreement or the parties. If the agreement primarily involves trade secrets and employees located in New York, and the recipient has minimal connections to California, a California choice of law provision may be deemed unenforceable, as it could be seen as an attempt to circumvent New York law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from New York to California. The perturbation explanation states that this may be invalid if there's no reasonable relationship between the contract and California, particularly if trade secrets and employees are in New York. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that under New York choice of law principles, the chosen state must have a reasonable relationship to the agreement. Since the snippets are missing, the determination relies on the Perturbation and Law explanations, which indicate a likely contradiction of legal principles regarding choice of law.
